records.pText:
<align="center">●In the Shadows of the Uprising, I</align>
Year 16 of the Eternian Ducal Calendar (Orthodox Calendar 2400)
This year, which has seen the world plunged into chaos with the appearance of the Great Chasm of Darkness, marks a full year since I had last visited this inn in Eternia.
The innkeeper handed me this small notebook when I first arrived. It seems I had forgotten it in my room during my last long stay, and she had held onto it for me.

I thanked her, and headed up to the familiar corner room on the second floor.
<align="center">* * *</align>
I set down my belongings and rolled onto the bed, flipping open these notes. The last entry must be a year prior, and the oldest some fourteen years ago.
I’m brought back to that Eternian street corner, the day after an unseasonably heavy snowfall.

<align="center">* * *</align>
Year 2 of the duchy (OC 2386), City of Eternia
Ominas Crowe, eighteen years of age. This man with an unabashedly disgruntled face had been complaining to me for some time. He wanted to know why, despite having been recognized for his meritorious service in the founding of the duchy, his name was listed nowhere in the program for “The Templar’s Uprising,” that dramatic oration popular at the port.

“This is a blemish on my credit! I was in the Vestment Cave with everyone else that day!” he whined, stamping his foot and stuttering all the while as he began his story.
<align="center">* * *</align>
2385
On what would later become the first year of the Eternian calendar, Braev the Templar and his like-minded comrades set off into the western skies aboard three airships.

The old man Yulyana would say to the families of those comrades along with the wounded soldiers like myself—that is, those incapable of fighting—that demons were coming from the east, and we were to hide in the Vestment Cave.
We would later learn that those “demons” were the Orthodoxy’s most sinister knights, the Bloodaxe Legion.

Becoming a knight of the Orthodoxy was any boy’s dream, but in contrast, the Bloodaxes were feared far and wide as merciless butchers. That three hundred of those butchers were accompanying the inquisitor meant the Orthodoxy intended to slaughter every last member of the templar’s faction and their families. * * *
That night, I, along with the templar’s wife Mahzer, their daughter, and the families of everyone else, took shelter in the cave.

They came with the dawn behind them. Their enormous airship landed in the lake, and one after another after another, the Bloodaxes disembarked and surrounded the old man’s house.
The inquisitor questioned Yulyana for days regarding the templar’s ingratitude. When it became clear to them that the insurgents were no longer in the area and it seemed they would turn their attention to the whereabouts of the insurgent’s families, Yulyana and Lord DeRosso dealt with them evasively.

By the third day of our refuge, I began to worry and went to take a peek outside the cave.
My injuries? Y-yes, I was still on crutches with a hurt leg.
What are you cocking your head at!
Wh-who else was there to do it? I was the only one able to put up a fight.
<align="center">* * *</align>

As I approached the southern shore of the lake, I could sense overwhelming bloodlust from all around. A detachment of Bloodaxes were searching for the refugees.
I hurried east, opposite the way I came, but they detected my motive and no longer attempting to hide their movements, appeared before me, ready to kill.
There were at a minimum dozens of the crimson demons. The setting sun stained the skies much the same color.

<align="center">* * *</align>
How many times was I beaten, kicked, thrown…? My healing leg was long ago broken.
Through tears, I attempted to cast fire after fira, which vanished into the twilight.
“Look at that. Just like little fireworks!”
A Bloodaxe covered in scars—he appeared to be their commander—mocked me, and the surrounding others followed suit.

Then, through the demons’ vile sneering, I could hear a voice I knew coming from just below my line of sight!
“Indeed. Fireworks meant to alert me.”
Before anyone had noticed, a small figure now stood between me and the Bloodaxe commander!
“O-old man…”

“Good work, lad.”
With my blurred vision, I could just make out handfuls of the bloodaxes falling with each of Yulyana’s leaps and bounds.
It all looked like it was in slow motion…
<align="center">* * *</align>
“Retreat!”
Following the commander’s cry, the bloodlust I had felt before faded, and the forest was quiet.

A ways off, I could see great flames licking the sky.
“…They angered Lord DeRosso.”
Yulyana screwed up his eyes as he tended to my wounds. Whether his expression was a smile or a grimace, I could not tell.

<align="center">* * *</align>
Back to Year 2, Eternia…
Just as Ominas was wrapping up the tale of his role during the uprising, the orator on the corner nearby was delivering that first high note, the scene of crossing the mountains, to the audience’s roaring applause.
“I never knew of such events in the shadows of the uprising!”
At my look of astonishment, Ominas scratched the end of his nose in pride.

But this is far different from the reputation Ominas had as told to me by others. Trying to reconcile my image of that Ominas and the one before me boggles the mind.
Unable to bear the silence of my confusion, he quickly changed the subject (or so I thought).
“I wouldn’t be here today if not for Mahzer!”

Mahzer? The templar’s wife? What connection could she have to Ominas and the uprising?
The black mage rambled on with no regard for my puzzlement.
<align="center">* * *</align>
On that day in 2379, when I was eleven years old, I rescued some travelers in the snow beset by monsters.

The travelers were Mahzer and her servant. The servant was injured, so I brought the two of them back to my village.
<align="center">* * *</align>
My hometown was a tiny settlement among those along Eternia’s eastern passage, unique in that most of the villagers were mages. Mahzer came from a village two mountains away. Our villages were relatively nearby, but there wasn’t particularly any contact to speak of between the two.

<align="center">* * *</align>
On that day, I was on my way to the head temple of the Orthodoxy. I had secretly applied to take the exam at their academy of magic.
I have no regrets about stopping to save Mahzer—don’t mistake me—but the ensuing kerfuffle by doing so meant that my intentions of running away from home were exposed.

“You don’t need any academy! You can become a fine mage by training here in the village!”
Hearing my relatives and the villagers say such things when they never even tried to set foot outside the village drove me mad. My emotions got the better of me and I couldn’t say what I wanted to say. Like every time, I would lose my temper and grow combative, and they would effectively shut me down with rational arguments.

It was Mahzer who convinced those adults to see things another way.
“I was just on my way to the head temple as well. Unfortunately, my attendant is now injured and can no longer move. Would it be possible to have Ominas escort me there?”
My parents and relatives, however begrudgingly, allowed me to go thanks to the persuasion of the lady the next village over.

That was how Mahzer brought me out of my hometown.
<align="center">* * *</align>
The next year, the Great Plague descended upon Eternia’s east. The heretofore unknown disease that had thrown the world into disarray had now come for us.
Every village along the eastern passage, mine and Mahzer’s included, was wiped out…

<align="center">* * *</align>
The orator nearby had reached the climax of the templar’s uprising, and used his every talent putting the deeds at the Temple of Earth into words, but to me, it sounded like events taking place in another world.
Ominas had a proud look on his face, as if to say, “See what I meant?”
<align="center">* * *</align>
I did see. He owed the templar’s wife his very life.

That’s why he was willing to risk luring the enemy away even while injured.
“Bah, enough of this! I have other things to do!” he stammered.
The Orthodoxy’s magic academy to which Ominas belongs will become the ducal academy.
It didn’t occur to me what effect that change might have on Ominas’s “other things.”

“B-bye!” Off he ran down the still-snowy street.
The next street over, I could hear the orator trying to attract an audience with a different program.
I was only too anxious to hear the second act of Ominas’s “In the Shadows of the Uprising.”

<align="right">To Be Continued</align>

<align="center">●In the Shadows of the Uprising, II</align>
Year 6 of the duchy (OC 2390), Eternia, a waiting room in the Central Healing Tower
The room was illuminated by what precious little daylight this season provides, flooding in through the skylight.
She arrived in a wheelchair pushed by a nurse. Mahzer Lee, wife of the templar. Or perhaps it is more appropriate now to title her as Mrs. Grand Marshal.

She should be thirty years old. Her complexion is pale from having spent such a long time under hospital care, and her blonde, gossamer hair was pulled to the side.
<align="center">* * *</align>
“Ominas said that?”
Looking at her smile, I could tell she must be feeling better than usual today.

It had been four years since I interviewed Ominas Crowe. Due to the First Lady’s illness, my numerous applications to interview her had been continually turned down until now.
<align="center">* * *</align>
“Ominas rescued me twice that day. If anything, I owe him my life.”

She had been squinting into the afternoon sun, but turning to face me, she said, “It doesn’t look like you believe me.”
She laughed softly to herself, and placed her hand on her breast as if to confirm something.
“It seems today is a good day for me. Let’s talk some more.”
She continued her quiet story.

<align="center">* * *</align>
It was the year 2379, after the “Duel of the Central Passage,” as I hear people now call it, when the winner of that duel visited me in my village. What he had to say to me was completely contrary to the rumors, and after saying his piece, he left on a journey to train further in his martial arts.
What exactly did he say?
…Haha, that’s a secret.

I will say that he had on him what is now our family treasure, an Eternity bracelet.
What? Yes, I suppose it is a common souvenir. But originally it was a traditional Eternian accessory given as a sign of affection to one close to you, and wearing it was a promise of future happiness.

I received the bracelet from him, and with it, went to his dear friend, Braev the Templar.
…That was the sort of person whom Ominas rescued from monsters and escorted to the temple.
<align="center">* * *</align>
My departure would have been delayed without Ominas. I would have perished in the Great Plague before marrying my husband.

And our daughter, [PCF2], would have never been born.
On top of that, he risked his life during the uprising to protect me, [PCF2], and the families of all our allies.
Now have I convinced you? Ominas has done more for me than I could ever repay.
<align="center">* * *</align>
The First Lady suffered a fit of dry coughing and fell silent for a while to catch her breath.

It had become clear: Ominas indeed played a role as Mahzer’s benefactor in matters both public and private.
I wanted to know more about the templar’s uprising from Mahzer’s perspective behind the scenes and readied my pen for more questions, but was quietly rebuffed by the nurse behind her.
“It’s time to be going,” I was told. Observing small fits of coughing continuing to afflict the First Lady, the nurse brought visiting hours to an end.

“If I could have just a few moments more…”
As I attempted to follow after my interviewee, a boy with his face hidden by a thin, black cloth came between us.
“You were told it’s time to go, weren’t you? If you can’t understand words, then…!”
The boy released a pulse of darkness that pushed me back, and I could pursue my subject no further.
<align="center">* * *</align>

Several months after my interview with the First Lady…
“Can you be any more annoying!?”
Ominas Crowe was throwing a fit on an Eternian street corner (he is supposedly twenty-two years old by now).
The staff he carried looked finer than what he had before, but otherwise he appeared the same as four years earlier, with a no-good look in his eyes as always.

“Why should I have to put up with you thinking poorly of me!”
(And he was frighteningly perceptive of any insults potentially directed at him.)
<align="center">* * *</align>
“You went to interview Mahzer? Why you… Be more considerate of her condition!”
When I related the details of the interview to him, his demeanor was more self-satisfied.

“Even hearing that, though, she’s obviously helped me more.”
Ominas looked like he remembered something, and turned his gaze to the Central Healing Tower.
“It wasn’t just my village she freed me from, but the Orthodoxy’s prison, too.”
I hurriedly prepared my pen and notepad.

<align="center">* * *</align>
It was 2380. The Orthodoxy—their top clergy—had turned a blind eye to the Great Plague. Every checkpoint east of the head temple was closed to refugees, and they let the disease wipe out every last village in the eastern corridor. Mahzer’s village and mine included…
That’s right, I already told you this last time.

I was at the magic academy and fed up with the Orthodoxy’s utter lack of mercy or action. I joined a protest organized by my classmates, but they shut it down immediately and threw us into a prison for political dissidents. The next year, 2381, Braev lost all hope in the Orthodoxy. Despite his rank, he gathered similarly disillusioned comrades and they fled Eternia with their families. As you know, Eternia’s surrounded by peaks that’ve long limited people’s comings and goings.

Mahzer learned of us imprisoned students when the templar was taking his people over the mountains, and she begged him to turn back and free us. Supposedly the templar was incredibly reluctant to permit such a detour, but Mahzer would not give in and he relented. “What was the purpose of leaving your office if you can’t even help those young men and women?”
…Heh heh, just like her to say something like that.

<align="center">* * *</align>
Convinced, the templar reversed course and he along with the fencer Khint and others routed their pursuers, rendezvoused with Swordmaster Kamiizumi freshly back from his journey, and broke us students out of prison in no time flat.
“Will you come with us?”
I firmly gripped Braev’s hefty hand and stood up.

<align="center">* * *</align>
Ominas began to move furtively, stealing glances at someone who appeared to be from Eternian Central Command.
“…See? That makes a second time Mahzer saved my life.”
It looked like he was finished telling stories, as he was now mumbling to himself feverishly.

Apparently the talented middle of some three sisters would be matriculating to the ducal academy, and this prodigy had him apprehensive.
“B-bye!” He ran off, leaving me with the same hasty farewell as the last time.
With this, I had gathered all the information I was looking for—or so I thought.

<align="right">To Be Continued</align>

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（１）
records.pText:
<align="center">●Chasing a Setting Sun, I</align>
Year 15 of the duchy (OC 2399)
I found myself in front of Eternian Central Command. “Eternian Central Command,” also known as the Orthodoxy’s head temple up until eleven years ago. It was seized in the templar’s uprising and now serves as the center of government and the military for the Duchy of Eternia. Setting that aside, it takes gall to summon someone and then make them wait outside.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（１）
records.pText:
Just as a light snow began to fall, I could see the familiar figure of black mage Ominas Crowe descending the long stairs.
<align="center">* * *</align>
Last year, the grand marshal’s guard was disbanded, and a newly formed fourth division, the Knights of the Eternian Sky, was formed in its stead. Their commander was the knight Argent Heinkel, and his lieutenant, believe it or not, was Ominas.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（１）
records.pText:
He should be thirty-one this year. It was a while since we had last met, and although he now sported whiskers along his chin, he hardly projected the dignity of a lieutenant commander.
<align="center">* * *</align>
“Hey, you’re still researching that, right? Then you can have this.”
He handed me a single, small notebook.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（１）
records.pText:
<align="center">* * *</align>
The Sky Knights to which he was assigned were to be dispatched to Caldisla. Ominas found the notebook while cleaning out his dorm at the academy in anticipation of shipping out.
(He’s still staying in the dorms at thirty-one?)

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（１）
records.pText:
I opened the notebook he had handed to me. It was originally used for his notetaking on the study of magic, crammed full with neurotic chicken scratch.
Partway through, though, written on a page in big, bold letters: “RECORD OF STRIKE FORCE’S SORTIE.” He had documented their movements that day.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（１）
records.pText:
<align="center">* * *</align>
2385
Painted red by the setting sun, the tents of our comrades lined the shore of Lake Yulyana. We were in Yulyana Woods, a hiding place for those who opposed and fled the oppressive rule of the Crystal Orthodoxy.
“I wanted to join the strike force, too. If it wasn’t for this leg…”

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（１）
records.pText:
The last person I wanted to hear my venting heard me. Argent Heinkel, aged twenty-seven.
On and on about chivalry he would prattle, without a tactful bone in his body, and he was always harassing me.
“Ominas, since your injury forces you to stay behind, you’ll be in charge of recording the strike force’s valorous exploits!”

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（１）
records.pText:
He had absconded from Eisenberg two years earlier and somehow ended up here, joining our cause (which means I’ve been at this with Braev for longer, mind you!). “Yeah, I will!”
Heinkel marched off to the three airships parked in the woods, laughing at my too-loud reply.
<align="center">* * *</align>
The sheets used to camouflage the ships had been removed, and the maintenance engineers had begun their final inspections.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（１）
records.pText:
The ships’ pilots had already gathered.
• Pilot of the first ship: Hayreddin Barbarossa (aged twenty)
• Pilot of the third ship: Ciggma Khint, fencer (aged twenty-two)
• Copilot of the second ship: Barras Lehr, page (aged nine)
Heinkel would pilot the second ship. The four of them began their strategy meeting.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（１）
records.pText: The course would be maintained as planned, following a westerly route. The ships would be outfitted with boosters in Teykov Heavy Industries’ secret factory in Ancheim, and there they would rendezvous with Dr. Vincent S. Court, the foremost authority on crystal studies, and his young son. From there they would head west across the Sea of Corsairs, refueling at Grandship. Proceeding further west to Starkfort in Eisenberg, they would load up on ammunition and dump their spent fuel.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（１）
records.pText:
Finally, they would enter Eternia through a col to the south.
<align="center">* * *</align>
The engineers reported that the first ship’s engine was showing issues with the throttle. The pilots considered proceeding with two ships in a worst-case scenario, but that would severely impact their plans.
Everyone looked to Heinkel, whose eyes were closed in thought.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（１）
records.pText:
“…No, no good.”
He had run through several two-ship scenarios, but the result was always the same.
“Our insurgency is outmanned as it is. Taking all three positions at once is a non-negotiable condition.”
Those three positions were:
• The Temple of Earth
• The Head Temple of the Orthodoxy
• The City of Eternia

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（１）
records.pText:
“We can make a call on the engine output based on the final inspection at Starkfort. Should the situation demand it, Khint, let Barras aboard to pilot the third ship.”
A sliver of displeasure played on the fencer’s face.
“Don’t look so sour. This is just a contingency plan, and one that depends on some stunt-flying, so to speak; Barras is the only one reckless enough to pull it off.”

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（１）
records.pText:
“…Explain to me this contingency plan.”
To the stoic fencer’s request for an explanation, Heinkel made an expression like a brat about to pull a nasty prank.
“If the first ship doesn’t have enough power, we’ll just give her a little boost…with the third ship from below!”
Barbarossa, pilot of the first ship, banged his prosthetic anchor in delight.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（１）
records.pText:
“Gah hah hah hah! This will be a riot!”
Khint crossed his arms as he digested the plan. His voice became even lower than usual. “The third ship will hardly come out unscathed.”
“Well, boss! What do you want I should do!”
Barras didn’t seem phased at being assigned to this gamble.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（１）
records.pText:
“Hmph, there’s no two ways about it. If it comes to it, you do it!”
Heinkel ordered Barras to make sure the third ship made it over the mountains—it didn’t matter what happened to the ship as long as it landed Khint in Eternia.
“Once Khint’s unit has seized the city, you make for the head temple and help me secure that. Got it!?”

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（１）
records.pText:
Heinkel’s orders were bordering on the absurd, but Barras accepted them gleefully, certain it was a task only he could accomplish.
“Now, as for the critical matter of when exactly is H hour…”
Inside the Yulyana Woods Needleworks, the debate over the pros and cons of the strike was entering its third night.

<align="right">To Be Continued</align>

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
<align="center">●Chasing a Setting Sun, II</align>
2385, Yulyana Woods
As everyone’s eyes were on the needleworks, its door opened and three men emerged.
Braev Lee, the Templar (aged 30)
Sage Yulyana (aged ???)
Lester DeRosso (aged ??)

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
“The Orthodoxy seems to have swallowed the information you fed them, Sage—hook, line, and sinker.”
At Lord DeRosso’s words, the sage’s eyes narrowed and he made a menacing smile.
<align="center">* * *</align>
The swordmaster, Nobutsuna Kamiizumi (aged 30), went to greet the three. This man, so versed in all sword techniques as to truly deserve the title of swordmaster, was Braev’s closest comrade.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
Braev nodded quietly and said to the swordmaster in a calm voice, “Nobutsuna, Mahzer is inside. You should say your farewells.”
“I did that six years ago.”
“Heh. I heard as much from her. That it was a rather demanding farewell at that.”

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
“Haha… Hurry and brief everyone. You’ve kept us waiting for three days.”
I was the farthest from possibly understanding what it was they were talking about, but strangely, I could well see how absolutely they trusted one another.
<align="center">* * *</align>
The members of the strike force gathered before Braev. Every one of them waited expectantly for his briefing, fire burning in their eyes.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
Three more members came tardily.
The red mage Fiore DeRosa (aged 23)
The entrepreneur Erutus Profiteur (aged 29)
The pharmacist Qada (aged 31)
“You’re late, DeRosa!”
To Heinkel’s chiding, DeRosa shrugged his shoulders and played innocent.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
I had seen those three whispering in the shadows any number of times, as I had the particularly icy stare the fencer Khint regarded DeRosa with.
<align="center">* * *</align>
Braev delivered a speech that moved every heart in the strike force, dispelling their fears, easing their nerves; one by one their expressions turned intrepid.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
I wasn’t able to match their expressions. I was jealous and frustrated.
<align="center">* * *</align>
“Any members of the strike force who do not yet have asterisks, step forward.” The old man Yulyana called to everyone while waving his staff.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
Asterisks were these strange gems he had. Most of the members had already received one. Three members stepped forward to now obtain theirs.
Profiteur was given the merchant asterisk.
Qada was given the salve-maker asterisk.
Barras was given the monk asterisk.
All three looked happily at their stones.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
The fencer Khint, who had been looking at the reddening lake, was approached by Yulyana, who whispered, “That cold fire inside you… If you find you can no longer control it at some point, come to see me. I’ll give you a new asterisk.”
Khint, bewildered, thanked the sage and walked toward the third ship, which was preparing to launch.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
The sun dipped below the trees to the west and the lakeshore was enveloped in night.
The roar of the three airships’ engines drowned out all other noise, creating a contrasting sort of silence.
The comrades gallantly boarded the ships. Those with family did so more reluctantly, first saying their goodbyes.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
“Do you envy them, lad?”
Yulyana was standing next to me, watching the airships.
The third airship took off first.
“Does it matter? I’m stuck here with a broken leg!”
I didn’t answer the question, but simply replied why I was excluded from the strike force.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
“Heh, you’re the one who broke it when you threw a tantrum and kicked Barras in his leg. That’s one way to earn your wound decorations.”
The fact that he was saying it to console me and not scold me made it hurt all the more.
“L-leave me alone! I-I’m…”
The second airship took off to follow the third.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
Whether it was the engine or the heat of my emotions, I had trouble hearing the old man’s voice.
“Put out your hand, lad.”
I did as he said, and he wrapped my fingers around whatever he placed in it. He then returned to the needleworks.
It was the black mage asterisk I had so desperately longed for.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
“On Braev’s recommendation. Ominas, you’re to protect all their families, hear?”
The engines drowned out all other sound, but that I heard clearly.
<align="center">* * *</align>
Braev passed the sage and walked over to where I was. In the distance, I could see Mahzer carrying an infant.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
“I’m leaving them in your care.”
He patted me on the shoulder with his hefty hand and left for the first airship.
<align="center">* * *</align>
The first airship took off. All three were now airborne, their reflections in the lake shattered by their thrust, and they soon disappeared into the western skies, as if they were chasing the setting sun.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
<align="center">* * *</align>
Back to Year 15 of the duchy (OC 2399). I gently closed Ominas’s notebook.
There were more pages that followed, but their contents appeared to have been written some time after the uprising, painfully embellished with their author’s aspirational dramatizations, and wholly unreadable.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
To Ominas, eager to hear my impressions, I said, “This (first half) is a superb primary resource!” and left it at that.
<align="center">* * *</align>
The snow was falling heavier. Other sky knights appeared at the top of the steps to collect the blushing Ominas, who hurried up to meet them.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
“Let’s get a move on, Ominas!”
“And be quick about it… You’ve got some nerve making me wait out here in the cold.”
“Ominas! You have to hurry! Or we’ll leave you behind!”
“B-Barras, you! I’m eight years older than you! Learn to respect your elders!”

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
“Settle down, now. If you break your leg again, we really will leave you behind.”
“It’s cold! All of you hurry up before I put my boot down your throats!”
The knight Heinkel, the black mage Ominas, the white mage Holly, and the monk Barras.
At a glance, the Knights of the Eternian Sky seemed to be a churlish lot, but at the same time a strangely well-balanced crew.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
A rare group able to fit the exceedingly warped peg that is Ominas.
<align="center">* * *</align>
The snow was now coming down in earnest. Ominas and the other sky knights could no longer be seen on the steps of Central Command. I turned to make my way back to the inn.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
The airship carrying Ominas and the others could be seen flying off to the east.
<align="center">* * *</align>
I trudged through the freshly fallen snow going down the central corridor.
Was this finally enough material to cover the events related to the templar’s uprising? All that was left was to organize it back at the inn.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
Or no, I should let it marinate a bit and finish up “The Industrialization of Ancheim and the Rise and Fall of the Early Khamer Dynasty.” Or maybe…
As I continued down the snowy road, my mind turning over this and that, my stomach let out a growl.
“First, something to eat,” I muttered out loud to no one but myself and adjusted the bags on my back.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
<align="center">* * *</align>
“Mr. Historian, dinner will be ready soon,” the innkeeper called sweetly from downstairs.
I closed Ominas’s notebook. Upon the bed where I was supposedly lounging were now all my materials from that period, neatly arranged by year.
Yes, I can write something with this.

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
records.pText:
When I had holed up here last year, there was something missing that prevented me from getting to work writing, but now I could sense that things were coming together.
“Mr. Historian, we’re having your favorite ginger-simmered bear! Come get it while it’s hot!”
I left my room to go down to the dining hall. Already the second floor was filled with the rich aroma of ginger and sweet soy sauce.
<align="right">Syversen, Wandering Historian</align>

records.pTitle: 沈む日輪を追いかけて（２）
